A starter guide to what panel providers do and how to think about quality, fit, and risk.
Frame panel suppliers as a sample-quality decision rather than a procurement detail.

## What panel providers do

Panel providers supply respondents that match target definitions, incidence expectations, geography, quotas, and timing requirements.

## What to evaluate

- Feasibility and incidence realism
- Sample source transparency
- Fraud and speed control processes
- Ability to manage complex quota structures
- Experience with the category or target audience

## Practical caution

Low cost is rarely the only relevant criterion. A cheaper sample that creates noisy or biased data usually costs more once analysis and confidence are affected.

How to distinguish qualitative and quantitative approaches and when each one is appropriate.
Compare qualitative depth with quantitative measurement.

## Qualitative research

Qualitative work helps you explore language, meaning, motivations, and context. It is strongest when the goal is discovery, reframing, or understanding how people make sense of a category.

## Quantitative research

Quantitative work helps you size, compare, prioritize, and estimate. It is strongest when the goal is measurement, segmentation, modeling, or tracking.

## A useful rule

Use qual to shape the hypothesis. Use quant to test how widespread or commercially meaningful it is.

Qualitative research

Qualitative work helps you explore language, meaning, motivations, and context. It is strongest when the goal is discovery, reframing, or understanding how people make sense of a category.

Quantitative research

Quantitative work helps you size, compare, prioritize, and estimate. It is strongest when the goal is measurement, segmentation, modeling, or tracking.

A useful rule

Use qual to shape the hypothesis. Use quant to test how widespread or commercially meaningful it is.
A step-by-step view of how a typical research project moves from brief to reporting.
Outline the common phases in a research project.

## Typical project phases

1. Clarify the decision and write the research objectives.
2. Choose the approach, sample, and timeline.
3. Design the instrument or discussion guide.
4. Program, field, and monitor quality.
5. Clean, structure, and label the data.
6. Analyze the results against the original objectives.
7. Build a clear report with recommendations.

## Where projects usually go wrong

- Objectives are too broad
- Stakeholders want multiple incompatible outputs from one study
- Data cleaning is left too late
- Analysis answers interesting questions rather than decision questions

  5. Clean, structure, and label the data.
  6. Analyze the results against the original objectives.
  7. Build a clear report with recommendations.

Where projects usually go wrong

  • Objectives are too broad
  • Stakeholders want multiple incompatible outputs from one study
  • Data cleaning is left too late
  • Analysis answers interesting questions rather than decision questions
The types of data we deal with
A practical overview of structured, unstructured, attitudinal, and behavioral data in research.
Distinguish the main kinds of research data and what they are good for.

## Common data categories

- Quantitative survey data: structured, codable, easy to table and model
- Qualitative data: interviews, groups, diaries, and open ends
- Behavioral data: transactions, visits, clicks, and usage records
- Derived variables: segments, scores, weights, and modeled outputs

## Why the distinction matters

Different data types support different claims. Structured survey data is strong for measurement and comparison. Qualitative material is strong for depth and language. Behavioral data is strong for observed action but not always motivation.

Common data categories

  • Qualitative data: interviews, groups, diaries, and open ends
  • Behavioral data: transactions, visits, clicks, and usage records
  • Derived variables: segments, scores, weights, and modeled outputs

Why the distinction matters

Different data types support different claims. Structured survey data is strong for measurement and comparison. Qualitative material is strong for depth and language. Behavioral data is strong for observed action but not always motivation.
A practical definition of market research and the role it plays in decision-making.
Explain the function of market research in business decisions.

## Why it exists

Market research reduces uncertainty. It turns business questions into evidence, then turns evidence into decisions that can be explained.

## What it typically covers

- Understanding customer needs, attitudes, and behaviors
- Measuring awareness, usage, satisfaction, and preference
- Testing concepts, messages, products, and prices
- Prioritizing where to act and what trade-offs matter most

## A practical definition

In practice, market research is less about collecting opinions and more about designing a disciplined way to learn. Good studies connect a business decision, a sample, a questionnaire or discussion guide, an analysis plan, and a clear recommendation.

A practical definition

Keep charts readable, comparable, and presentation-ready without losing analytic meaning.
The chart is part analysis and part communication design.

## Useful defaults

- Start with the clearest chart type, not the most decorative one
- Keep axes and labels explicit
- Highlight the message, not every data point equally
- Reuse templates for consistency

Useful defaults

  • Start with the clearest chart type, not the most decorative one
  • Keep axes and labels explicit
  • Highlight the message, not every data point equally
  • Reuse templates for consistency
Set up crosstabs and banner structures so outputs remain consistent across analyses.
Strong banner discipline prevents messy reporting later.

## Working rules

- Define banner variables early
- Keep subgroup labels concise
- Use consistent base notes
- Check that small subgroups are still interpretable

Working rules

  • Define banner variables early
  • Keep subgroup labels concise
  • Use consistent base notes
  • Check that small subgroups are still interpretable
A practical sequence for cleaning and checking survey data before analysis.
Keep cleaning decisions explicit and reversible.

## Cleaning sequence

- Check sample size and missingness
- Validate key screeners and quotas
- Review open-ended and numeric outliers
- Confirm multi-punch and derived variable logic
- Document every recode that affects reporting

Cleaning sequence

  • Check sample size and missingness
  • Validate key screeners and quotas
  • Review open-ended and numeric outliers
  • Confirm multi-punch and derived variable logic
  • Document every recode that affects reporting
A simple structure for moving from raw data to a clean, explainable analysis workflow in Displayr or Q.
A starter workflow checklist for Displayr and Q projects.

## Suggested workflow

1. Confirm the business question and outputs needed.
2. Review the raw file structure and metadata.
3. Clean, label, and organize variables before analysis.
4. Set weights and banner definitions early.
5. Build crosstabs, significance checks, and charts from a stable base.
6. Save reusable outputs and reporting structures.

Suggested workflow

  1. Confirm the business question and outputs needed.
  2. Review the raw file structure and metadata.
  3. Clean, label, and organize variables before analysis.
  4. Set weights and banner definitions early.
  5. Build crosstabs, significance checks, and charts from a stable base.
  6. Save reusable outputs and reporting structures.
Move from analysis outputs to a clear client-facing story without losing technical integrity.
Reporting should connect the evidence back to the original decision.

## Reporting flow

1. Restate the decision and objective.
2. Surface the few findings that matter most.
3. Use charts to support the argument.
4. Add caveats where they genuinely affect interpretation.
5. End on implications and recommended actions.

Reporting flow

  1. Restate the decision and objective.
  2. Surface the few findings that matter most.
  3. Use charts to support the argument.
  4. Add caveats where they genuinely affect interpretation.
  5. End on implications and recommended actions.
A starter reminder for when and how to use significance testing in tabular outputs.
Use significance testing to support interpretation, not replace judgment.

## Practical reminders

- Check assumptions before running tests automatically
- Avoid turning every table into a sea of significance markers
- Use significance to support the narrative, not become the narrative

Practical reminders

  • Check assumptions before running tests automatically
  • Avoid turning every table into a sea of significance markers
  • Use significance to support the narrative, not become the narrative
Keep labels, groups, recodes, and derived variables organized so analysis remains readable.
Clean naming and grouping pay off across the entire project.

## Practical guidelines

- Use clear variable labels and short names
- Separate raw variables from derived variables
- Group related questions together
- Freeze naming conventions before heavy charting starts

Practical guidelines

  • Use clear variable labels and short names
  • Separate raw variables from derived variables
  • Group related questions together
  • Freeze naming conventions before heavy charting starts
Starter notes for setting up, checking, and documenting survey weights.
Weighting should be documented as a design decision, not a hidden cleanup step.

## Key checks

- Confirm target distributions and source
- Inspect extreme weights
- Compare weighted and unweighted outputs on important variables
- Record where weighting meaningfully changes interpretation

Key checks

  • Confirm target distributions and source
  • Inspect extreme weights
  • Compare weighted and unweighted outputs on important variables
  • Record where weighting meaningfully changes interpretation
A feature or dimension included in a research design, especially in conjoint or choice studies.
Attributes define what varies in an exercise or model. In conjoint they are the dimensions respondents trade off, such as price, package size, or support level.
Attributes define what varies in an exercise or model. In conjoint they are the dimensions respondents trade off, such as price, package size, or support level.
A set of subgroup columns used in crosstab output for comparison.
A banner organizes subgroup views in a table so the same analysis can be compared across audiences, brands, waves, or demographics.
A banner organizes subgroup views in a table so the same analysis can be compared across audiences, brands, waves, or demographics.
A group of cases that are more similar to each other than to cases in other groups.
Clusters are the building blocks of many segmentation solutions. The technical grouping step still needs interpretation and action planning to become useful.
Clusters are the building blocks of many segmentation solutions. The technical grouping step still needs interpretation and action planning to become useful.
A trade-off based method used to estimate the value of product features and price levels.
Conjoint is used when the business decision depends on how respondents trade features and price against one another, rather than rating each one in isolation.
Conjoint is used when the business decision depends on how respondents trade features and price against one another, rather than rating each one in isolation.
A glossary entry for incidence rate.
The proportion of people who qualify for a study based on screening criteria.
Incidence rate affects feasibility, fieldwork cost, and timeline. Low incidence audiences require more careful planning and supplier discussion.
Incidence rate affects feasibility, fieldwork cost, and timeline. Low incidence audiences require more careful planning and supplier discussion.
A glossary entry for latent variable.
An underlying construct that is inferred from observed responses rather than measured directly.
Many useful research concepts, such as attitudes or needs, are latent. We infer them through patterns across observed questions or behaviors.
Many useful research concepts, such as attitudes or needs, are latent. We infer them through patterns across observed questions or behaviors.
A pool of recruited respondents available to participate in research studies.
A panel can come from one or multiple sources. Quality evaluation matters because sample source and fraud controls affect the reliability of the resulting data.
A panel can come from one or multiple sources. Quality evaluation matters because sample source and fraud controls affect the reliability of the resulting data.
A statistical indication that an observed difference is unlikely to be due to random variation alone under a stated model.
Significance is useful, but it does not automatically mean a difference is large, important, or commercially meaningful.
Significance is useful, but it does not automatically mean a difference is large, important, or commercially meaningful.
A modeled value representing the relative attractiveness of an item or level within a choice framework.
Utilities are relative, not literal emotional reactions. They help compare options within the same modeled system.
Utilities are relative, not literal emotional reactions. They help compare options within the same modeled system.
Adjusting the contribution of cases so the sample better reflects a target population or design requirement.
Weighting is often necessary, but it changes how results should be interpreted. Always check where weighted and unweighted views diverge meaningfully.
Weighting is often necessary, but it changes how results should be interpreted. Always check where weighted and unweighted views diverge meaningfully.

Estimate how people trade off product features, levels, and price when making choices.
Conjoint is a structured choice-modelling approach used to estimate the value respondents place on different feature levels by asking them to make trade-offs.
When you need to understand trade-offs across multiple attributes
When product design or offer configuration is the core decision
When price needs to be evaluated alongside non-price features
When respondents cannot realistically evaluate the attribute set
When the category is too unfamiliar for stable trade-offs
When you only need a quick directional price sense
A well-defined attribute and level structure
Sample size aligned to segmentation and simulation needs
Compare three subscription plans that vary in price, support level, and reporting features, then estimate which combinations drive uptake.
Forces realistic trade-offs instead of isolated ratings
Connects well to product and pricing decisions
Can become cognitively heavy if too many attributes are included
Results depend on realistic levels and task framing

I use conjoint when the real decision is configuration, not just preference ranking. The key is to keep the design commercially plausible and define upfront which market scenarios the client actually needs to simulate.
Look at utility direction and relative spacing
Treat attribute importance as design-specific not universal truth
Focus on simulation outputs for business decisions

## Why it matters

Conjoint is often the strongest choice when stakeholders need to make product design trade-offs and want to understand the cost of adding or removing features.

## What to watch closely

The commercial usefulness depends more on design quality than on analysis sophistication. Unrealistic attributes produce polished but weak outputs.

This method is often best used to understand the structure of the problem before a final operational clustering step.

Partition observations into a fixed number of clusters by minimizing within-cluster variation.
K-means is a clustering algorithm that allocates each case to one of K clusters based on distance to a cluster centroid.
When you already have a likely range for the number of clusters
When variables are numeric and standardized
When you need a relatively efficient baseline segmentation algorithm
When the data has strong outliers or non-spherical structure
When the number of clusters is completely unknown
When interpretability requires a more exploratory clustering path
Multiple random starts or stability checks
Run a four-cluster solution on standardized need-state batteries to create a first segmentation candidate.
Using raw variables with different scales
Choosing K only because it looks neat in a presentation
I use K-means as a candidate generator rather than an automatic answer. It becomes useful once combined with stability checks and strong segment profiling.

Position it as one way to derive a segmentation solution not the whole story

Estimate relative preference strength across a list of items by asking respondents to choose the best and worst.
MaxDiff measures relative preference or importance by forcing respondents to choose the most and least appealing items from repeated sets.
When you need a stronger ranking than simple ratings
When the item list is important but full trade-off modeling is unnecessary
When attribute combinations rather than standalone items matter
Rank value propositions to determine which messages deserve front-page emphasis in a campaign.

## Decision rule

If the question is about acceptable range start lighter. If it is about demand or configuration trade-offs use stronger methods.

If the question is about acceptable range start lighter. If it is about demand or configuration trade-offs use stronger methods.
